title: Cyrus the Great founds the Achaemenid Empire
summary: Around 550 BCE Cyrus II of Anshan, until then a Persian vassal of the Median king Astyages, overthrew his overlord, captured the Median capital of Ecbatana, and welded the Median and Persian realms into a single Achaemenid state. Within twenty years he had added Lydia and its Greek client cities, and in 539 BCE the ancient city of Babylon. The empire he assembled ran from the Aegean to the Hindu Kush and was, for its time, the largest political unit the world had yet seen.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The Iranian plateau in the seventh century BCE was dominated by two related but distinct peoples, both speaking dialects of Old Iranian. In the north-west the Medes, based on the fertile Hamadan plain, had risen to power under kings such as Cyaxares, who around 612 BCE joined the Babylonian Nabopolassar in sacking Nineveh and ending the Assyrian empire. In the south the Persians, whose small kingdom of Anshan sat in the Fars highlands above the head of the Persian Gulf, were subject to Median suzerainty. Cyrus II inherited the throne of Anshan around 559 BCE as the fourth ruler of a line his own inscriptions traced to a certain Achaemenes, or Hakhamanish, from whom the empire took its later dynastic name.

The wider Near East at that moment was carved into four great powers. The Median empire ran from central Anatolia to the eastern Iranian plateau. The Neo-Babylonian empire of Nabopolassar and his son Nebuchadnezzar II, who had destroyed Jerusalem and deported its elite in 587 BCE, held Mesopotamia and the Levant. The Lydian kingdom of Alyattes and his son Croesus, whose capital at Sardis controlled the Anatolian trade routes and whose electrum coinage was the earliest true state currency, held western Asia Minor. Egypt under the Twenty-Sixth Dynasty at Sais ruled the Nile valley from the Delta to Elephantine.

Between these four powers passed a long, uneasy peace, sealed by dynastic marriages. Astyages of Media, according to Herodotus, was Cyrus's own maternal grandfather; the Median princess Mandane had been given in marriage to the Persian king Cambyses I precisely to bind the two royal houses. The founding legends preserved in Herodotus's Histories, with the exposed infant Cyrus rescued by shepherds and the prophetic dreams of Astyages, are patterned on wider Near-Eastern hero stories and probably grew up in the generation after Cyrus's death; the underlying political relationship, of a Persian vassal house tied by marriage to the Median court, is solid enough. What is beyond dispute, from the contemporary Babylonian Nabonidus Chronicle, is that in his sixth regnal year, around 550 BCE, King Astyages marched against Cyrus and was defeated when his own army mutinied and handed him over.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The core narrative comes from the Nabonidus Chronicle, a Babylonian cuneiform tablet compiled soon after the events it records. According to its terse entry, Astyages assembled his army and advanced on Cyrus of Anshan; the Median troops rebelled, seized their king, and delivered him to Cyrus. Cyrus then marched on the Median capital of Ecbatana, sacked the palace, and carried its silver, gold and other goods back to Anshan. Herodotus adds the detail that a Median noble, Harpagus, whom Astyages had years earlier tricked into eating the flesh of his own son as punishment for sparing the infant Cyrus, was the architect of the mutiny. Whatever the personal drama, the political result was that in a single campaign Cyrus took over the whole Median empire and its subject peoples.

He did not, however, simply overthrow the older kingdom. Cyrus retained Median administrative structures and personnel, kept Ecbatana as one of his royal residences, and styled himself successor to Astyages in the north as well as king of Anshan in the south. This dual identity of Median-Persian rule became the template for Achaemenid kingship. From about 550 BCE onwards contemporary sources refer to the empire simply as that of the Medes and Persians, or by the Old Persian term for the ruling house, Hakhamanishiya, from which the modern name Achaemenid derives.

Having inherited Media, Cyrus moved outward. Around 547 BCE, or slightly later according to a revised reading of the Nabonidus Chronicle, he defeated Croesus of Lydia at the battle of Pteria in Cappadocia and pursued him back to Sardis, where he captured the city after a siege on its steep acropolis. Herodotus reports that at the decisive engagement in front of Sardis Cyrus placed his baggage camels ahead of his cavalry; the Lydian horses, unused to the scent, panicked, and the Lydian charge broke. With Sardis fell the Greek cities of Ionia along the Aegean coast, and with them, in time, the seed of the later Greco-Persian Wars.

His last great campaign was against Babylon. Nabonidus, the Babylonian king, had alienated much of the Marduk priesthood by promoting the moon god Sin and by spending years in the Arabian oasis of Tayma. In October 539 BCE Cyrus's forces under his general Ugbaru defeated a Babylonian army at Opis on the Tigris and entered Babylon itself on 12 October without resistance; Cyrus made his own ceremonial entry on 29 October. The Cyrus Cylinder, a baked clay barrel inscription later deposited in the walls of the Esagila temple, records that Cyrus, presenting himself as the chosen instrument of Marduk, restored displaced peoples and their gods to their homes. The Hebrew Bible, in Ezra and Second Isaiah, remembers him as the anointed one, mashiach, who ended the Babylonian exile and permitted the Judahite deportees to return to Jerusalem and rebuild the Temple.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Cyrus's empire was administered through a network of provinces, later formalised under his successors as the satrapy system. Each satrap governed a large region on the king's behalf, collected tribute, and maintained a standing garrison; parallel royal secretaries and military commanders reported independently, discouraging revolt. A royal road system, most famously the Royal Road from Sardis to Susa described by Herodotus and confirmed by later itineraries, tied the provinces together with mounted couriers who covered the 2,500-kilometre route in about a week. Aramaic, already the trade language of the Near East, became the empire's chancery language, written in a variant of the Phoenician alphabet.

Cyrus himself died in 530 BCE, on campaign in the far north-east against the nomadic Massagetae beyond the Syr Darya, if the account of Herodotus can be trusted; alternative traditions have him die in bed. His body was carried back to Pasargadae, the ceremonial capital he had begun to build in the Persian heartland, and interred in a modest limestone gable-tomb that still stands. His son Cambyses II, on succeeding him, extended the empire further by conquering Egypt in 525 BCE at the battle of Pelusium, adding the Nile to Persian domains.

Under Cyrus's more distant successors, Darius I (r. 522 to 486 BCE) and Xerxes I (r. 486 to 465 BCE), the Achaemenid empire was consolidated and monumentalised. Darius codified the tribute system in the twenty satrapies described in Herodotus's third book, standardised coinage with the gold daric and silver siglos, and built the new palace complex of Persepolis. His war with the Greek cities, provoked in part by the Ionian Revolt and Athenian intervention at Sardis, ended in defeat at Marathon in 490 BCE and again at Salamis and Plataea a decade later. Yet even these reverses were peripheral. The empire itself, running from the Aegean and Egypt to the Indus, endured for two centuries.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The Achaemenid Empire founded by Cyrus set the pattern for every later imperial project in western Asia. Alexander the Great, who destroyed it in a single decade from 334 to 330 BCE, consciously took over its satrapies, its road system, and much of its ceremonial. His Seleucid and later the Parthian and Sasanian successors kept the same broad framework of a Persian-Iranian core ruling a mosaic of tributary peoples through a Aramaic-writing bureaucracy. The Roman and Byzantine empires that shared the Near East with Sasanian Iran learned the same lesson at second hand.

Cyrus's personal reputation was extraordinary and unusual for a conquering emperor. Greek writers as different as Herodotus and Xenophon (in the fictionalised Cyropaedia) treated him as a model ruler, and it was through them that his image reached Renaissance and Enlightenment Europe. Thomas Jefferson owned two copies of Xenophon's work and drew from it, and from the Cyrus Cylinder as reported in ancient sources, elements of a governance ideal that reappears in the American founding.

Within the Jewish and later Christian traditions Cyrus occupies a unique place as the only non-Israelite figure called mashiach in the Hebrew Bible, the anointed one of the Lord who ended exile and permitted the rebuilding of the Temple. His edict, however it was worded in reality, became a template for later imperial policies of religious toleration.

The Cyrus Cylinder itself, excavated by Hormuzd Rassam in 1879 in the ruins of Babylon and now in the British Museum, has become a modern political symbol as well as an ancient one. It was declared by the last Shah of Iran in 1971 the world's first charter of human rights, a description modern historians treat with caution but which reflects the enduring pull of Cyrus's reputation as a ruler who chose, more often than not, to leave conquered peoples with their gods, their languages and their customs intact.

worldContext.text: In mainland Greece the sixth century BCE was the age of the tyrants and the early lawgivers. In Athens, the reforms of Solon in 594 BCE had reshaped the constitution, and Peisistratus and his sons ruled from 561 BCE as popular strongmen. Sparta had by now completed its conquest of Messenia. Ionian Greek cities on the Anatolian coast, freshly incorporated into Cyrus's empire after the fall of Sardis, were producing the first natural philosophers: Thales, Anaximander and Anaximenes at Miletus, whose questions about the underlying stuff of the world begin the Greek scientific tradition.

In China this was the Spring and Autumn period of the Eastern Zhou. The Zhou king at Luoyang was a ceremonial figure, real power lying with rival states such as Qi, Jin, Qin and Chu. Confucius (Kongzi, traditionally 551 to 479 BCE) was born in the state of Lu around the year of Cyrus's Median campaign; a slightly older contemporary, Laozi, is traditionally credited with founding the Daoist tradition. Iron tools and coinage were beginning to spread through the Yellow River valley.

In the Andes the Chavín cult centred on the highland shrine of Chavín de Huántar was still influential; on the Peruvian coast the Paracas culture was producing its extraordinary embroidered burial textiles. In Mesoamerica the Olmec civilisation of the Gulf Coast had entered its late phase, while at Monte Albán in the Valley of Oaxaca the Zapotec city was being founded and its earliest platforms and carved danzante reliefs raised on the ridge above the valley floor.

In Egypt the Twenty-Sixth or Saite Dynasty at Sais in the western Delta ruled a wealthy independent state whose pharaohs, notably Amasis II (Ahmose II, r. 570 to 526 BCE), pursued a policy of alliance with mainland Greek cities and settled Greek and Carian mercenaries at Naukratis and Elephantine. Their independence would end in 525 BCE when Cyrus's son Cambyses II crossed the Sinai. Further south, in modern Sudan, the Kushite kingdom now centred on Meroe was developing its own script and iron-working economy.

In the western Pacific the Lapita cultural horizon of the previous millennium had given way to distinct ancestral Polynesian and Melanesian societies. In western Polynesia, communities in Tonga and Samoa were consolidating the social hierarchies, canoe technology and horticultural practices that would in later centuries carry their descendants further east across the ocean. In Australia, Aboriginal societies maintained the world's oldest continuous cultural tradition, adapting to local environments across the continent.

figures.name: Cyrus II the Great
figures.role: King of Anshan from c. 559 BCE and founder of the Achaemenid Empire from c. 550 BCE until his death on campaign in 530 BCE

figures.name: Astyages
figures.role: Last king of the Median Empire, defeated and dethroned by Cyrus around 550 BCE

figures.name: Croesus
figures.role: King of Lydia in western Anatolia, defeated by Cyrus at Sardis in the late 540s BCE

figures.name: Nabonidus
figures.role: Last king of the Neo-Babylonian Empire, dethroned when Cyrus's forces entered Babylon in October 539 BCE

images.caption: The Cyrus Cylinder, a baked clay barrel inscribed in Akkadian cuneiform and deposited in the walls of the Esagila temple in Babylon after Cyrus's conquest in 539 BCE. British Museum, London.
images.credit: Photograph by Prioryman, 2012, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Cyrus the Great permits the return of the Jewish exiles from Babylon to Jerusalem. Manuscript illumination by Jean Fouquet, c. 1470, in a French copy of Josephus's Antiquities of the Jews. Bibliothèque nationale de France, Paris.
images.credit: Jean Fouquet (c. 1420 to 1481), via Wikimedia Commons
